explanation: Flutter requires a positive integer flex value; a value of zero causes an assertion error.

text: In the tutorial, why did the tutor use EdgeInsets.all(30) inside the containers?
optionA: To make the containers take up the whole screen.
optionB: To provide uniform internal spacing (padding) for the containers.
optionC: To set the flex factor to 30.
explanation: EdgeInsets.all(30) creates a 30-pixel buffer inside the container so the text doesn't touch the edges.

text: If you want a widget to take up 75% of a Row and another to take up 25%, what flex values should you use?
optionD: Both A and B are correct.
explanation: Whether you use 75 and 25 or 3 and 1, the ratio remains the same (3:1), resulting in the same visual size.

text: What is the result of putting a Column inside an Expanded widget that is inside a Row?
optionA: The Column will expand horizontally to fill the Row's available width.
optionB: The Column will expand vertically to fill the screen height.
optionD: The Column will shrink to zero size.
explanation: Because the parent is a Row, the Expanded widget controls the horizontal width, even if the child is a Column.

text: When the tutor added a large Image inside an Expanded widget, what was the visual result?
optionB: The image was scaled down to fit the available space.
optionC: The image remained large and caused an error.
optionD: The background color of the Row changed.
explanation: By wrapping the image in Expanded, it is forced to fit within the layout boundaries instead of overflowing.

text: Can you nest an Expanded widget inside another Expanded widget?
optionB: No, it will cause a layout error unless there is a Flex widget (Row/Column) in between.
optionC: Yes, but only in landscape mode.
optionD: No, Expanded widgets must be top-level.
explanation: You cannot nest an Expanded directly inside another; there must be a Row or Column to define the new layout direction.

text: Which property is NOT part of the Expanded widget?
optionD: All of the above are part of Expanded.
explanation: Expanded is purely a layout widget. Visual properties like color belong to widgets like Container or SizedBox.

text: If you have a Row with MainAxisAlignment.start and two Expanded widgets, where does the 'leftover' space go?
optionA: To the left of the widgets.
optionB: To the right of the widgets.
optionC: There is no leftover space; Expanded widgets consume all of it.
explanation: Expanded widgets are 'greedy' — they grow until they have used up every bit of available space on the main axis.

text: In the Ninja ID project, what is the primary role of the Scaffold widget's structural properties versus the MaterialApp widget's home property?
optionA: The MaterialApp's home property defines the root widget tree, while Scaffold provides the structural layout (AppBars, Drawers).
optionB: The Scaffold's home property determines the app bar, while MaterialApp's home determines the body.
optionC: Both are interchangeable depending on whether the widget is stateful or stateless.
optionD: MaterialApp handles the background color, while Scaffold handles the title text.
explanation: MaterialApp is the top-level configuration for the whole app, whereas Scaffold provides specific layout slots like appBar and body for a single screen.

text: When setting the elevation property of an AppBar to 0, what visual effect is being specifically targeted?
optionA: It makes the AppBar transparent so the body shows through.
optionB: It removes the drop shadow to create a flat design.
optionC: It aligns the title text to the absolute top of the screen.
optionD: It removes the padding between the AppBar and the status bar.
explanation: Elevation represents the Z-axis (height). Setting it to 0 puts the AppBar on the same level as the background, removing the shadow.

text: In a Column widget, if you use CrossAxisAlignment.start, how does it compare to MainAxisAlignment.start?
optionA: CrossAxis aligns children horizontally (left), while MainAxis aligns them vertically (top).
optionB: Both center the children by default if the other is not defined.
optionC: The app would throw a runtime error because the axes are incompatible in a Column.
optionD: Nothing would change because 'start' is the default for both axes.
explanation: In a Column, the Main Axis is vertical (Top-to-Bottom) and the Cross Axis is horizontal (Left-to-Right). Setting MainAxisAlignment affects vertical positioning.

text: What is the specific advantage of using a SizedBox for spacing between widgets rather than wrapping every widget in a Padding widget?
optionA: SizedBox automatically scales the gap based on screen resolution.
optionB: SizedBox can only be used inside a Row, whereas Padding is for Columns.
optionC: It allows for a fixed, invisible gap without adding deep nesting levels to the widget tree.
optionD: SizedBox is a stateful widget while Padding is stateless.
explanation: SizedBox is a standalone widget used to create precise gaps. Using Padding for every gap would result in deep nesting, making code harder to read.

text: To create the circle avatar, the tutorial uses CircleAvatar with a backgroundImage. Why is AssetImage('path') used instead of just a raw string path?
optionA: The backgroundImage property requires an ImageProvider object, not a raw string.
optionB: AssetImage automatically compresses the file size for the avatar.
optionC: Using a string path directly only works for images hosted on the web.
optionD: A string path is only valid if the image is stored in the lib folder.
explanation: Properties like backgroundImage cannot read a String directly; they need an ImageProvider like AssetImage or NetworkImage.

text: The Divider widget is given a height of 60. How does this height actually manifest in the UI?
optionA: It makes the actual horizontal line 60 pixels thick.
optionB: It sets the width of the line to 60 pixels from the left margin.
optionC: It creates 60 pixels of total vertical space, with the line centered within that space.
optionD: It forces the Divider to occupy 60% of the screen height.
explanation: The height property of a Divider defines the height of the transparent box the divider sits in, not the thickness of the visible line.

text: In the pubspec.yaml file, what is the critical step after adding an asset path to ensure the app can find the image?
optionA: Converting the image to a .png format for compatibility.
optionB: Running the 'Packages get' or 'Get dependencies' command.
optionC: Restarting the physical computer or emulator.
optionD: Indenting the asset path by exactly four spaces.
explanation: Simply saving the pubspec.yaml file isn't enough; you must run this command to tell Flutter to update its internal asset manifest.

text: If a Row contains an Icon and Text, and the MainAxisAlignment is set to spaceEvenly, what happens?
optionA: The icon and text would be pushed away from each other towards the edges of the Row.
optionB: The icon would move to the top and the text to the bottom.
optionC: The icon and text would remain touching but move to the exact center.
optionD: The Row would disappear because spaceEvenly requires at least three children.
explanation: spaceEvenly distributes free space equally between, before, and after children. With two items in a Row, they will be pushed far apart toward the left and right sides.

text: What happens if you omit the child property in a Padding widget?
optionA: The Padding will take up the entire screen.
optionB: It will default to an empty Container widget.
optionC: The code will show a compiler error because the child property is required.
optionD: The padding will be applied to the parent widget instead.
explanation: The Padding widget is a wrapper; its only job is to provide space for a child. Without a child, Flutter marks it as a required parameter.

text: In the TextStyle property, what does letterSpacing: 2.0 specifically do?
optionA: It adds 2 pixels of space between words only.
optionB: It adds 2 logical pixels of space between each individual character.
optionC: It doubles the width of every letter in the string.
optionD: It increases the line height by 2 pixels.
explanation: letterSpacing increases the horizontal gap between letters (kerning), a common design choice for headings.

text: Why use Colors.grey[850] and Colors.grey[900] instead of just Colors.grey?
optionA: Because standard Colors.grey is not compatible with dark mode.
optionB: To create a subtle visual contrast between the App Bar and the main background.
optionC: The square brackets are required to activate the color's visibility.
optionD: To ensure the text on top remains perfectly white.
explanation: Using slightly different shades of the same color creates visual hierarchy, making the AppBar appear distinct from the Scaffold body.

text: What is the mandatory entry point for any Flutter application?
explanation: In Dart, the main() function is the mandatory entry point. The app cannot start without this function calling runApp().

text: In the CircleAvatar, what does the radius property define?
optionA: The thickness of the border around the image.
optionB: The sharpness of the image corners.
optionC: The distance from the center of the circle to its edge (half the diameter).
optionD: The total width of the widget in percentage.
explanation: The radius is exactly half the width of the circle. A radius of 40 results in a CircleAvatar that is 80 pixels wide and 80 pixels tall.

text: If you want to add a background color to a specific Text widget, what is the best approach?
optionA: Set the color property of the Text widget to two different values.
optionB: Wrap the Text widget in a Container and set the Container's color.
optionC: Use the backgroundColor property inside the TextStyle widget.
optionD: Text widgets automatically inherit the background color of their parent Column.
explanation: A Text widget has no 'background' property. To add color or a border around text, you must place it inside a Container or DecoratedBox.

text: What does the EdgeInsets.fromLTRB constructor stand for?
explanation: fromLTRB follows a specific order for its four parameters: Left, Top, Right, and Bottom.

text: What is the function of the Center widget when wrapping another widget?
optionA: It centers the child widget horizontally and vertically within its parent's available space.
optionB: It centers the text alignment inside a child Text widget.
optionC: It makes the child widget's size relative to the screen.
optionD: It is a required wrapper for all Image widgets.
explanation: The Center widget takes up all available space and positions its child in the exact middle. It is used to center the CircleAvatar on screen.

text: Inside a Column children list, what happens if a String is added directly instead of a Widget?
optionA: Flutter automatically wraps the String in a Text widget.
optionB: The code will show a compiler error because the list only accepts Widget types.
optionC: The String will be ignored and not displayed.
optionD: The String will be displayed as raw code on the screen.
explanation: Flutter's children lists are strictly typed as List<Widget>. Since a String is not a Widget, the code is type-invalid and will not compile.

text: What is the purpose of using Colors.amberAccent[200] in a dark-themed ID card app?
optionA: To provide a high-contrast color for important data values.
optionB: It is the required color for all number-based text.
optionC: To act as the background color for the email icon.
optionD: It is used to define the shadow of the AppBar.
explanation: The app uses gray for labels (e.g., 'NAME') and AmberAccent for the specific data (e.g., 'Chun-Li') to make important information pop visually.

text: In a SizedBox used for horizontal spacing within a Row, which property is essential?
explanation: Inside a Row, items are side-by-side. A SizedBox needs width to create a horizontal gap; height would create vertical space that wouldn't separate the items.

text: What does the 'stless' shortcut do in modern IDEs like VS Code or Android Studio?
optionA: It removes all state from the current file.
optionB: It converts a Stateful widget into a Stateless one.
optionC: It generates a boilerplate template for a new Stateless widget class.
optionD: It stands for 'Style Less' and removes all colors from the UI.
explanation: stless is a Live Template or snippet that saves time by automatically generating the class, build method, and Widget return statement.

text: Why does Flutter split a Stateful Widget into two distinct classes?
optionA: To allow the UI to be compiled separately from the logic.
optionB: Because the Widget class must remain immutable (unchanging), while the State class holds the mutable data.
optionC: It is a legacy requirement from the Java/Android framework.
optionD: To ensure that one class handles styling and the other handles positioning.
explanation: Flutter widgets are temporary blueprints that are destroyed and rebuilt constantly. The State object is the only part that persists to store dynamic data.

text: Which method is the entry point where a StatefulWidget 'spawns' its associated logic container?
explanation: createState() is the link between the two classes. When a StatefulWidget is added to the UI tree, it calls this method to create its State object.

text: If you change a variable's value outside of setState(), what is the state of the framework?
optionA: The Framework enters an 'Error State' and stops rendering.
optionB: The internal data changes, but the widget is not marked as 'dirty,' so the screen doesn't repaint.
optionC: The Framework automatically detects the change via 'Auto-Watch' and updates the UI.
optionD: The app crashes with a NullPointerException.
explanation: Flutter is reactive. While a variable's value in memory updates, the screen only redraws when you call setState(), which signals the framework to run the build method again.

text: Where does the build method reside once a widget is converted to Stateful?
explanation: In a StatefulWidget, the build method moves to the State class so it can access the variables being updated by the logic.

text: What is the performance impact of calling setState()?
optionA: It only rebuilds the specific line of code that changed.
optionB: It re-downloads the entire app package from the store.
optionC: It triggers the build method, recreating the widget tree starting from that widget downward.
optionD: It has zero impact because Flutter is always rebuilding 60 times per second regardless.
explanation: setState() marks the widget as 'dirty,' triggering the build method again and recreating the widget tree from that widget downward.

text: Which of these is a valid use case for a StatefulWidget?
optionA: Displaying a static image that never changes.
optionB: A text label that displays 'Hello World' permanently.
optionC: A checkbox that needs to remember if it was clicked.
optionD: A button that only navigates to a new page without changing its own appearance.
explanation: If a widget needs to 'remember' a choice or value (like a box being checked), it must be Stateful.

text: In Dart string interpolation, how would you correctly display an integer variable named score?
explanation: The $ symbol is the Dart operator for String Interpolation — it is the most efficient way to inject a variable's value directly into a text string.

text: When performing a 'Hot Restart,' what happens to the variables in your State class?
optionA: They keep their last modified value.
optionB: They are reset to their original initialization values.
optionC: They are deleted, and the app must be reinstalled.
optionD: They are saved to the phone's local storage automatically.
explanation: A Hot Restart fully wipes the app's current memory. Unlike a Hot Reload, it starts the app fresh from the beginning, resetting all variables.

text: What is the 'Ninja Level' variable type used in the tutorial?
explanation: Whole numbers (1, 2, 3...) are stored as integers (int). The ninja level is a whole number, so int is the correct type.

text: What happens if you call setState() inside the initState() method?
optionA: It works perfectly and is the standard way to load data.
optionB: It triggers an error because the widget hasn't been 'mounted' to the tree yet.
optionD: It speeds up the initial loading of the app.
explanation: initState() happens during the very first creation of the widget. You cannot tell a widget to 'refresh' its UI before it has finished being placed on the screen.

text: Which property of the Scaffold widget is used to place the floating button shown in the video?
explanation: The Scaffold widget provides a specific property called floatingActionButton for the standard Material Design action button.

text: To create a button that increments a value, you must pass a function to which property?
explanation: onPressed expects a function to run. If you set onPressed: null, the button is automatically disabled and greyed out by Flutter.

text: In the tutorial, why was Colors.grey[800] used instead of just Colors.grey?
optionA: To change the opacity of the button.
optionB: To select a specific shade or strength of grey from the material color swatch.
optionC: To make the button compatible with older Android phones.
optionD: It was a syntax error that the instructor corrected later.
explanation: Material colors are defined in swatches from 50 (lightest) to 900 (darkest). The [800] index selects a specific deep, dark shade.

text: What is the purpose of the Icon widget's Icons.add property?
optionB: To display a '+' glyph from the Material Icons font.
optionC: To add a new widget to the screen.
optionD: To increase the size of the button.
explanation: Icons are specialized font characters. Icons.add refers to the specific glyph in the Material library that represents the plus symbol.

text: If your UI has a Column, and one item inside it changes size during setState(), what happens to the other items?
optionA: They stay in their original positions and overlap.
optionB: They disappear until the app is restarted.
optionC: They shift automatically to accommodate the new size of the changing widget.
optionD: They are deleted from the widget tree.
explanation: Flutter uses a Flex layout engine. If one widget in a Column gets bigger, the engine instantly recalculates the space for every other widget.

text: Which operator is a shorthand for ninjaLevel = ninjaLevel + 1?
explanation: ninjaLevel++ is the shorthand 'increment' operator. It takes the current value, adds 1, and saves the new value back in one step.

text: Why is the State class usually prefixed with an underscore (e.g., _NinjaCardState)?
optionA: It makes the code run faster.
optionB: It marks the class as 'private,' meaning it can only be used within that specific file.
optionC: It tells Flutter that this is a 'Stateless' class.
optionD: It is a required symbol for all classes in Dart.
explanation: In Dart, an underscore (_) before a name makes it library-private. This is a security feature called encapsulation.

text: What is the return type of the build method?
explanation: The build method is a function that must return an object of type Widget — the basic building block Flutter uses to draw the interface.

text: In the tutorial, which command did the instructor use to 'hard refresh' the data values?
explanation: The instructor used Hot Restart specifically to demonstrate that it is the only way to reset the data counters back to their starting values during testing.

text: What is the main disadvantage of making every widget a StatefulWidget?
optionA: The app will not run on iOS.
optionB: It adds unnecessary memory overhead and complexity to widgets that do not need to change.
optionC: You cannot use colors in Stateless widgets.
optionD: It makes the font size smaller.
explanation: Stateful widgets are 'heavier' because they track state over time. Making them Stateless when possible saves memory and makes the app faster.

text: What is the primary purpose of the map() method in Flutter?
optionA: To delete items from a list
optionB: To transform a list of data into a list of widgets
optionD: To create a new background color for the app
explanation: map() iterates through a data collection and returns a new collection of results, in this case widgets.

text: In the video, which data type was initially used to store the list of quotes?
explanation: Initially, a simple List<String> was used before moving to a custom class.

text: Why is it necessary to use .toList() at the end of a map() function in Flutter?
optionA: To make the code run faster
optionB: Because the children property of a Column expects a List, not an Iterable
optionC: To save the data to a database
optionD: To change the font size of the text
explanation: map() returns an Iterable. Since children requires List<Widget>, .toList() performs the conversion.

text: Which widget was used to display the list of quotes vertically?
explanation: A Column layout widget arranges its children in a vertical array.

text: What is a 'class' in the context of this Flutter tutorial?
optionB: A blueprint for creating objects with specific properties
optionC: A specific screen in the app
explanation: Classes allow developers to define custom data types with multiple properties and methods.

text: What problem does creating a custom Quote class solve?
optionA: It allows you to group related data (like text and author) together
optionB: It makes the app icon look better
optionC: It automatically translates the app into different languages
optionD: It reduces the battery consumption of the app
explanation: Using a class lets you treat the quote text and its author as a single logical unit.

text: How do you define a constructor in a Dart class?
optionB: By creating a function with the same name as the class
explanation: The constructor is the special method called when a new object of that class is created.

text: What do curly braces { } around parameters in a constructor indicate?
explanation: Named parameters improve code readability by requiring the parameter name when passing arguments.

text: In the expression quotes.map((quote) => ...), what does 'quote' represent?
optionB: A single item from the list as it is being iterated
optionC: The index number of the item
explanation: Inside the map function, the argument represents the current element being processed.

text: Which property of the Text widget is used to display the actual string?
explanation: The first argument passed to a Text widget is the string that will be displayed.

text: Where should the custom Quote class definition usually be placed?
optionB: Outside of any other classes or in a separate file
explanation: Keeping class definitions top-level or in separate files makes the code more modular and organized.

text: What is the benefit of using the fat arrow (=>) syntax in the map function?
optionA: It is a shorthand for a function that returns a single expression
optionC: It is used to import new libraries
optionD: It defines a variable that cannot be changed
explanation: The => syntax is a concise way to write functions that only contain one return statement.

text: If you have a Quote object named myQuote, how do you access its author property?
explanation: Dot notation is used in Dart to access the properties or methods of an object.

text: Which widget was suggested to wrap the text to give it a better visual structure?
explanation: A Card widget provides a material design surface with rounded corners and a shadow.

text: In the tutorial, how were the initial quotes defined?
optionA: They were fetched from a website
optionB: They were typed into a hardcoded list of strings
optionD: They were read from a text file
explanation: The instructor created a static list within the code to demonstrate the mapping logic.

text: What happens if you try to pass a list of Strings to a property that expects a list of Widgets?
optionD: The text appears as code on the screen
explanation: Dart is a type-safe language; you cannot pass a List<String> where a List<Widget> is required.

text: What is the purpose of the initState() method?
optionB: To initialize data when the widget is first created
explanation: initState() is the place where you set up the initial state of a StatefulWidget.

text: Which keyword is used to create a new instance of a class in Dart?
explanation: While new is the keyword, Dart allows you to omit it for cleaner code in modern versions.

text: How do you display a variable inside a String in Dart (String Interpolation)?
explanation: The dollar sign is used to insert the value of an expression directly into a string literal.

text: If you wanted to add a 'delete' feature to the list later, why is the map() method useful?
optionC: It allows the UI to react dynamically to changes in the underlying data list
optionD: It speeds up the deletion process
explanation: By mapping the data to widgets, any change in the data list can be reflected in the UI when setState is called.

text: What is the primary purpose of the map() method in Flutter?
optionA: To delete items from a list
optionB: To transform a list of data into a list of widgets
optionD: To create a new background color for the app
explanation: map() is used to cycle through data and convert each entry into a visual component.

text: Why is it necessary to use .toList() at the end of a map() function inside a Column?
optionA: To make the code run faster
optionB: Because the children property expects a List, but map() returns an Iterable
optionC: To save the data to a database
optionD: To change the font size of the text
explanation: map is a lazy operation in Dart; toList() converts it into the concrete collection format Flutter requires.

text: Which data type is best for grouping related info (like a quote and its author) together?
explanation: Classes allow you to define multiple properties (types) under one name, unlike a simple String.

text: In the code quotes.map((quote) => Text(quote)).toList(), what does the variable 'quote' represent?
optionB: A single item from the list as it is being iterated
optionC: The index number of the item
explanation: The parameter in a map function represents the individual element currently being processed.

text: How do you define a constructor with named parameters in Dart?
explanation: Curly braces {} denote named parameters in Dart constructors.

text: Which widget is used to provide an elevated, box-like look with shadows to list items?
explanation: The Card widget is a Material Design component specifically for elevated surfaces.

text: What is the benefit of moving a custom class into its own separate file?
optionA: It is required for the app to compile
optionB: It keeps the code organized and makes the class reusable
explanation: Modularizing code into separate files (e.g., quote.dart) is a best practice for maintainability.

text: What does the => (fat arrow) represent in Dart?
optionB: A shorthand for a function that returns a single expression
optionC: A pointer to a memory address
optionD: An instruction to move to the next screen
explanation: Also called 'fat arrow' syntax, it makes one-line return functions cleaner.

text: How do you access a property (like author) from a class instance named q?
explanation: Dart uses dot notation to access properties of a class instance.

text: What import is required at the top of a file to use standard Flutter widgets?
explanation: This package contains the basic UI building blocks of Flutter.

text: What does the 'required' keyword in a constructor ensure?
optionB: The property cannot be null and must be provided
optionC: The data is saved to the cloud
optionD: The property is a private variable
explanation: required prevents runtime errors by ensuring necessary data is passed to the constructor.

text: Which widget adds space around the outside of a Column or Row?
explanation: Padding creates internal or external breathing room for widgets.

text: Which property of a Card widget controls the depth of its shadow?
explanation: Higher elevation values result in a larger, softer shadow.

text: What is the 'DRY' principle mentioned in coding practices?
explanation: DRY encourages using reusable widgets or functions instead of copying code.

text: If you have 50 items in a list, how many widgets does map() produce?
explanation: map performs a 1:1 transformation for every item in the original list.

text: What is the result of using forEach() inside a Column's children property?
optionB: It causes a compiler error because it returns void
explanation: forEach executes code but doesn't return a list of widgets to the UI; it returns void.

text: How do you display a variable inside a string (String Interpolation)?
explanation: This syntax allows you to embed logic or variable values directly into text.

text: Why extend StatelessWidget for a simple data template like a Quote Card?
optionA: Because the data inside the card does not need to change once built
optionB: Because it is faster than a class
optionC: Because it uses less internet data
optionD: Because it is required for shadows
explanation: If the UI is static (not updating in real-time), a StatelessWidget is more efficient.

text: Which widget is used to create a vertical gap between two Card widgets?
explanation: A SizedBox with a defined height is the standard way to add space between widgets in a Column.

text: What does map() return before you call .toList()?
explanation: An Iterable is a collection of elements that can be accessed sequentially, which map() produces by default.

text: What is the fundamental difference between a StatelessWidget and a StatefulWidget?
optionA: StatelessWidget can only be used for text.
optionB: StatefulWidget allows the widget's appearance to change based on internal data (state) during its lifetime.
optionC: StatelessWidget does not have a build method.
optionD: StatefulWidget is only used for buttons.
explanation: Stateless widgets are static; stateful widgets are dynamic and can update based on internal data.

text: In Flutter, what is the definition of 'State'?
optionA: The physical location of the user.
optionB: Information that can be read synchronously when the widget is built and might change over time.
optionC: The current version of the Flutter SDK.
optionD: A list of all files in the project directory.
explanation: State is basically the data the app uses to determine what the UI looks like at any given moment.

text: Which method must be called to update the UI when a variable's value changes in a StatefulWidget?
explanation: setState() is the specific function that tells Flutter 'something changed, please redraw this widget.'

text: Why does a StatefulWidget require two separate classes?
optionA: One for Android and one for iOS.
optionB: To separate the immutable widget definition from the mutable state logic.
optionC: Because it makes the app run twice as fast.
optionD: It is a legacy requirement from older versions of Dart.
explanation: The Widget class is destroyed and rebuilt often; the State class persists to keep track of data.

text: What happens when setState() is called?
optionB: Flutter ignores the call unless it's in debug mode.
optionC: The framework schedules a rebuild of the widget and its children with the new values.
optionD: The background color of the app changes to white.
explanation: Calling setState() marks the widget as 'dirty,' triggering the build method again.

text: Which lifecycle method is called exactly once when the widget is first inserted into the widget tree?
explanation: initState() is the first method called in the State lifecycle, used for one-time initialization.

text: Where should you ideally initialize data or start listeners in a StatefulWidget?
explanation: Since initState only runs once, it's the perfect place to set up data without repeating logic.

text: Which method is called right before a widget is permanently removed from the widget tree?
explanation: dispose() is used for cleanup, such as closing data streams or canceling timers.

text: In the tutorial's 'Ninja ID' app, what specific state was being tracked and displayed?
optionB: A numeric counter representing the 'Ninja Level.'
optionD: A list of the Ninja's equipment.
explanation: The level was incremented by 1 every time the user clicked a button.

text: What is the purpose of the createState() method in a StatefulWidget?
optionB: To return an instance of the associated State class.
explanation: createState() connects the 'Widget' part to the 'Logic/Data' part of the stateful pair.

text: Why is it bad practice to put complex logic or data initialization inside the build() method?
optionB: The build() method can be called many times, leading to performance issues or logic errors.
explanation: Because build is called every time the UI updates, you do not want to restart heavy processes there.

text: Which class is used as the base for the class that contains the build() method in a stateful setup?
explanation: The State class is generic and must be linked to the specific Widget it manages.

text: What is the visual result of incrementing a variable without calling setState()?
optionB: The variable changes in the background, but the UI continues to show the old value.
explanation: Data changes do not automatically trigger UI changes; Flutter needs the explicit setState() signal.

text: What shortcut can you use in VS Code or Android Studio to generate a StatefulWidget template?
explanation: stful is a standard code snippet to save time writing boilerplate code for a StatefulWidget.

text: Which widget was used in the video to trigger the increment of the 'Ninja Level'?
explanation: A round FloatingActionButton at the bottom of the screen was used to 'level up' the Ninja.

text: The initState() method is part of which class?
explanation: Lifecycle methods like initState, build, and dispose are all part of the State class.

text: How many times is the build() method called during a widget's lifecycle?
optionC: Potentially many times, whenever the state changes.
optionD: Only when the app is first opened.
explanation: Every call to setState() triggers the build() method.

text: What does 'immutable' mean in the context of the StatefulWidget class itself?
optionA: The class can be changed anytime.
optionB: The properties of the widget class cannot change once it's created; only the State object can hold changes.
optionC: The widget cannot be seen on the screen.
explanation: Widgets are lightweight blueprints; the State object is the long-lived storage that holds mutable data.

text: Which lifecycle method follows immediately after initState() is completed?
explanation: Once initialized, Flutter immediately calls build() to display the initial state.

text: In the tutorial, the variable ninjaLevel was defined as what type?
explanation: The level was a whole number (0, 1, 2...), which is an integer (int) in Dart.

text: What is the main benefit of 'Extracting' a widget in Flutter?
optionA: It makes the app run faster on older hardware.
optionB: It improves code readability and allows for reusability.
optionC: It is the only way to change the background color.
optionD: It reduces the number of files in your project.
explanation: Extracting widgets cleans up giant build methods and lets you use the same UI component in multiple places.

text: When you extract a widget into its own class, what does it usually extend?
explanation: Custom widgets are built by extending the existing base widget classes provided by Flutter.

text: In the tutorial, why was the QuoteCard widget created?
optionA: To handle the app's navigation logic.
optionB: To act as a template for displaying individual quotes.
optionC: To store the list of data.
explanation: Instead of repeating the Card/Column/Text logic for every quote, QuoteCard handles the display for any quote passed to it.

text: How do you pass data into an extracted widget?
optionC: By saving the data to a text file first.
optionD: You cannot pass data into extracted widgets.
explanation: Constructors are the gateway to passing data from a parent widget to a child widget.

text: Which keyword is used in the constructor to ensure a property is assigned a value when the widget is created?
explanation: Since Dart 2.12 (Null Safety), 'required' ensures the widget has the data it needs to function correctly.

text: What is the standard way to declare a property inside a Stateless widget that will hold passed-in data?
explanation: This tells Flutter exactly what kind of data the widget expects and that the data will not change during the widget's current life.

text: Why is the 'final' keyword used for properties inside a StatelessWidget?
optionB: Because StatelessWidgets are immutable; their data should not change after they are built.
optionC: It allows the variable to be modified later.
optionD: It is required by the Android operating system.
explanation: If you need to change data within a widget, you should use a StatefulWidget instead.

text: If you have an extracted widget named QuoteCard and a variable q, how do you instantiate it using a named parameter?
explanation: If the constructor uses curly braces { }, you must use the parameter name when passing the value.

text: What happens if you forget to include a required parameter when calling an extracted widget's constructor?
optionA: The app runs but the widget is invisible.
optionB: The app crashes only on iOS.
optionC: The compiler will throw an error and the app will not build.
optionD: Flutter uses a random default value.
explanation: Null safety ensures that you cannot forget mandatory data, preventing crashes at runtime.

text: What is the purpose of 'this.quote' in a constructor like QuoteCard({required this.quote})?
optionA: It refers to a global variable.
optionB: It automatically assigns the passed argument to the class property named 'quote'.
optionC: It tells Flutter to rebuild the entire app.
optionD: It is a comment for other developers.
explanation: This is syntactic sugar in Dart to avoid writing this.quote = quote inside a function body.

text: Where is the best place to define your extracted widget class?
optionA: Inside the build method of another widget.
optionB: In the same file (below the main widget) or in a separate .dart file.
explanation: Keeping widgets in separate files is best for large projects; keeping them at the bottom of the same file is okay for small examples.

text: When refactoring code, what does the 'Extract Widget' shortcut in IDEs do?
optionB: It automatically generates the class structure and constructor for the selected widget tree.
optionC: It uploads the code to GitHub.
optionD: It converts the app into a website.
explanation: Most IDEs have a 'Refactor' tool that automates the tedious parts of extracting a widget.

text: In the tutorial, which widget was used inside the QuoteCard to create the 'card' effect?
explanation: The Card widget provides the rounded corners and drop shadow seen in the tutorial.

text: What is the benefit of using named parameters in a widget constructor?
optionA: It makes the code harder to read.
optionB: It allows you to pass arguments in any order and makes the call site more descriptive.
optionC: It is required for all final variables.
optionD: It allows the widget to be stateful.
explanation: QuoteCard(quote: myQuote) is much clearer than QuoteCard(myQuote), especially when there are multiple parameters.

text: If your extracted widget needs to perform a function (like deleting a quote), how should you handle it?
optionA: Write the logic inside the extracted widget.
optionB: Pass a function as a parameter (callback) to the extracted widget.
optionC: Use a Navigator to go to a new screen.
explanation: This allows the child widget to notify the parent when an action (like a button press) occurs.

text: What does the build method of an extracted widget return?
optionC: An Integer representing the pixel count.
explanation: Every widget must implement a build method that returns what should be shown on the screen.

text: How do you access the properties of a custom widget inside its build method?
optionB: By using the property name directly (e.g., quote.text).
optionD: Properties are not accessible inside the build method.
explanation: Since the properties are members of the class, they are available throughout the build method.

text: What is the primary reason for passing a key to the super constructor?
optionA: To change the color of the widget.
optionB: To help Flutter uniquely identify widgets in the widget tree for efficient updates.
optionD: It is only used for StatefulWidgets.
explanation: Keys are especially important when dealing with collections of widgets that can move or be deleted.

text: In the video, what did the instructor use to map the list of quotes to the new QuoteCard widget?
explanation: .map() converts the list of data objects into a list of QuoteCard widgets.

text: Extracted widgets help follow which software engineering principle?
explanation: Extracting widgets prevents you from writing the same UI code multiple times, making updates much easier.
